1.synthesis:
Naaman, commander of the army of the king of Aram, is a great man and in favor with his master, a mighty man of valor — but he is a leper (v. 1). Through a raiding party, an Israelite girl has been taken as a servant to Naaman’s wife; she says: would that my lord were with the prophet who is in Samaria! He would cure him of his leprosy (vv. 2–3). The chain of testimony moves from the servant girl to Naaman to the king of Aram to the king of Israel, whose tearing of clothes at the letter reveals his terror: am I God, to kill and make alive, that this man sends word to me to cure a man of his leprosy? (v. 7). Elisha sends word: let him come now to me, that he may know that there is a prophet in Israel (v. 8).

Naaman arrives with horses and chariots — the full military escort of a great man — and stands at the door of Elisha’s house (v. 9). Elisha does not come out; he sends a messenger with instructions: go and wash in the Jordan seven times, and your flesh will be restored and you will be clean (v. 10). Naaman is furious: he expected the prophet to come out, stand, call on the name of YHWH, wave his hand over the spot, and cure the leprosy. Are not Abana and Pharpar, the rivers of Damascus, better than all the waters of Israel? Could I not wash in them and be clean? (vv. 11–12). He turns away in rage. His servants approach him carefully: if the prophet had commanded you to do something great, would you not have done it? How much more when he says, wash and be clean? (v. 13). Naaman goes down and dips in the Jordan seven times; his flesh is restored like the flesh of a little child (v. 14).

Naaman’s story is the paradigmatic narrative of divine humbling. He is great in every earthly category — favor, military achievement, valor — and his greatness is the obstacle to his healing. The humbling comes in three stages: the prophet does not come out personally (he is not important enough to YHWH’s representative to merit a personal audience); the prescribed action is bathetically ordinary (wash in the Jordan); and the Jordan is specifically inferior by Naaman’s own assessment to the rivers of his own country. Each stage strips away another layer of Naaman’s confidence in his own standing, until only the seven-fold obedience to an apparently demeaning command remains as the path to healing.
1.historical_context: Naaman’s leprosy (likely a broader category of skin disease in the biblical period than modern Hansen’s disease) is described as an affliction that exists alongside his status and military role — he is still a functioning general, not quarantined or socially excluded. This suggests it was a manageable but disfiguring condition. The Israelite servant girl’s testimony is the narrative’s most remarkable element: a prisoner of war, taken in a raid, witnesses to her captor’s general’s healing available from Israel’s God through Israel’s prophet. Luke 4:27 cites Naaman’s healing as evidence that YHWH’s grace extends beyond Israel’s boundaries, a claim that enraged the Nazareth synagogue audience.
1.literary_structure: 2 Kings 5:1–14 is structured around three escalating humiliations for Naaman and one act of faith: Elisha sends a messenger (does not come out); the instruction is bathetically simple (wash, not some dramatic gesture); the Jordan is inferior to Damascus’s rivers; and the healing comes only after all pride is relinquished. The servants’ gentle reasoning in v. 13 — “if he had commanded you to do something great” — is the narrative’s interpretive comment: Naaman’s pride expects greatness to require great acts. The kingdom of God operates differently: small obedience, large healing. The Jordan, not the Abana or Pharpar, is the site of renewal.
1.theological_application: Naaman’s expectation — the prophet should come out, perform an impressive gesture, and heal through dramatic ceremony — is the expectation of every person who assumes that access to divine power must match the scale of their social standing. He brought enough silver and gold to compensate a prophet generously; he expected a performance proportionate to his gifts and his rank. The simple instruction to wash in an ordinary river humiliated him precisely because it denied the relevance of everything that made him great. YHWH’s healing does not require human greatness, impressive ceremony, or proportionate compensation. It requires obedience to instructions that may seem inadequate, repeated until the prescribed number of times is complete. The healing comes not in the impressive gesture but in the seventh dip in the unremarkable river.
1.christology: Naaman’s flesh restored “like the flesh of a little child” (v. 14) images the new creation: the great man becomes like a child, stripped of the greatness that was his pride and given the cleanness that only the Jordan’s simple washing could provide. Jesus’ citation of Naaman’s healing in Luke 4:27 — “there were many lepers in Israel in the time of the prophet Elisha, and none of them was cleansed, but only Naaman the Syrian” — identifies the inclusive grace of YHWH that bypasses the covenant nation to reach the despised foreigner. Naaman the Syrian is the type of every Gentile who is included in the covenant promises not by being Israel but by humble obedience to the word of Israel’s God. The Jordan where Naaman was cleansed is the same Jordan where Jesus was baptized — the water of humble obedience that identifies the covenant community regardless of ethnicity.

15.synthesis:
Naaman returns to Elisha with all his company and stands before him: behold, I know that there is no God in all the earth but in Israel (v. 15). His confession is the theological climax of the narrative — a Syrian general making a monotheistic confession of the God of Israel. He offers Elisha a gift; Elisha refuses it: as YHWH lives, before whom I stand, I will receive none (v. 16). Naaman presses him and he refuses. Then Naaman makes two requests: permission to take two mule-loads of Israelite soil, so that he may offer burnt offerings and sacrifices to YHWH only on Israelite ground (v. 17); and forgiveness in advance for the times when he must accompany his master the king of Aram into the house of Rimmon and bow there — not because he worships Rimmon but because his royal duty requires it (v. 18). Elisha’s reply is simply: “Go in peace” (v. 19).

Naaman’s confession — “there is no God in all the earth but in Israel” — directly inverts the question Elijah had asked about Ahaziah (1:3): the Syrian general who has been healed has no doubt that there is a God in Israel. His request for Israelite soil reflects the ancient Near Eastern assumption that gods are geographically localized; he wants to worship YHWH on technically Israelite ground in Syria. Elisha’s acceptance of this request without correction suggests a pastoral latitude toward the newly converted who are working out the implications of a new theological commitment in the context of their existing obligations and cultural framework.
15.historical_context: The request for Israelite soil for worship reflects a common ancient Near Eastern understanding that deities were territorially bound; carrying the soil of YHWH’s land to Syria would create a kind of portable sanctified space for Naaman’s worship. The request is theologically naive from an Israelite monotheistic perspective — YHWH does not require Israelite soil to be worshipped — but Elisha does not correct it, perhaps because the theology will develop and the sincerity is genuine. Rimmon (also called Hadad) was the storm deity of Damascus, the national god of Aram. Naaman’s pre-emptive request for forgiveness about bowing in the temple of Rimmon is a sophisticated piece of conscience navigation: he is not asking permission to worship Rimmon but acknowledging that his royal duties will require him to perform a physical act that looks like worship without being worship in intent.
15.literary_structure: 2 Kings 5:15–19 is Naaman’s response to healing — a sequence of three acts: confession (v. 15), refused gift (vv. 15–16), and two requests with Elisha’s response (vv. 17–19). The refused gift is Elisha’s way of ensuring that the healing is unambiguously YHWH’s gift, not a commercial transaction between a prophet and a wealthy foreign general. The two requests bracket the complexity of Naaman’s new faith: he wants to worship YHWH truly (the soil request) while navigating existing obligations honestly (the Rimmon request). Elisha’s “go in peace” — shalom, the same word the Shunammite used when her son was dead — is a sending word that commissions Naaman back to his life with the new theological identity he has received.
15.theological_application: Elisha’s refusal of Naaman’s gift and his acceptance of Naaman’s pastoral situation without demanding immediate full compliance with every implication of monotheism models the kind of pastoral wisdom that receives genuine faith where it is and does not require it to have arrived at its final destination. Naaman is genuinely converted — his confession is real — but his understanding of what that conversion requires is incomplete. Elisha does not correct his theology of soil or refuse him peace because of the Rimmon situation; he receives the genuine confession, refuses the commercial gift, and sends him away in peace. Communities that demand theological completeness as the entry requirement for pastoral acceptance will not receive the Naamans who come with genuine confession and incomplete understanding. The “go in peace” is the mission-sending word that trusts YHWH to develop what Elisha has begun.
15.christology: Naaman’s confession — “there is no God in all the earth but in Israel” — is the monotheistic confession that becomes, in the new covenant, the confession of Jesus as Lord. The Syrian general who was outside the covenant arrives at YHWH-only confession through an act of physical humbling (seven dips in the Jordan), not through theological instruction. This pattern is the pattern of conversion throughout Acts: the encounter with YHWH’s power in the life of the Spirit precedes and generates theological understanding rather than following from it. Elisha’s refusal to take payment for the healing models the principle that the gift of God cannot be purchased (Acts 8:20 — “may your silver perish with you, because you thought you could obtain the gift of God with money”). The healing is YHWH’s alone to give and Elisha’s alone to refuse payment for.

20.synthesis:
Gehazi, servant of Elisha the man of God, says to himself: my master has spared this Naaman the Syrian, in not accepting from his hand what he brought. As YHWH lives, I will run after him and get something from him (v. 20). He runs after Naaman, who leaps down from the chariot to meet him; Gehazi lies: my master has sent me to say, two young prophets from the hill country of Ephraim have just come to me; please give them a talent of silver and two changes of clothing (vv. 21–22). Naaman gives him two talents and sends two servants with him to carry them; Gehazi hides the silver in the house and sends the servants away (vv. 23–24).

He goes to stand before his master; Elisha asks: where have you been, Gehazi? He says: nowhere. Elisha says: did not my heart go with you when the man turned from his chariot to meet you? Is it a time to accept money and garments and olive orchards and vineyards and sheep and oxen and male and female servants? The leprosy of Naaman shall cling to you and to your descendants forever (vv. 25–27). Gehazi goes out leprous, like snow.

The Gehazi episode is the dark mirror of the Naaman episode. Naaman, the outsider, came with a diseased body and left healed, confessing YHWH as the only God; he offered money and Elisha refused it. Gehazi, the insider — Elisha’s own servant — was present for all of this and immediately ran to extract the money that Elisha had refused. The leprosy that left the Syrian general clings to the Israelite servant: the outsider’s disease becomes the insider’s inheritance, because the outsider humbled himself while the insider betrayed his master’s witness. The reversal is complete and the irony is devastating.
20.historical_context: Gehazi appears earlier in the Shunammite narrative as Elisha’s servant (ch. 4) and will appear again in 8:4–5, apparently still serving in some capacity (unless the reference is to an earlier time). The two talents of silver that Gehazi receives represent an enormous sum — far exceeding any normal transaction. Elisha’s description of what Gehazi might have bought — olive orchards, vineyards, sheep, oxen, male and female servants — maps out an entire estate-level investment, not a modest supplement to a prophet’s income. The escalation is Elisha’s way of naming the spirit of avarice that motivated Gehazi’s action: it was not need but greed, not modest supplement but empire-building at the expense of a convert’s integrity.
20.literary_structure: 2 Kings 5:20–27 is structured as a mirror-inversion of the Naaman healing: where Naaman came to Elisha in need and left healed, Gehazi follows Naaman in greed and returns cursed. The phrase “as YHWH lives” that Gehazi uses (v. 20) echoes the same oath Elisha used to refuse the gift (v. 16); Gehazi invokes YHWH’s name to legitimate his act of greed, which is itself a profanation. Elisha’s “my heart went with you when the man turned from his chariot” (v. 26) is prophetic knowledge of the secret transaction — the same prophetic knowledge that the Shunammite’s crisis had been “hidden from me” (4:27). What YHWH hides from the prophet about innocent suffering, he reveals fully about servant betrayal.
20.theological_application: Gehazi’s betrayal is the failure of the insider who uses privileged access for personal gain. He had seen Elisha’s miracles, had been entrusted with the prophet’s most intimate ministry (the Shunammite’s son), and had observed Elisha’s refusal of Naaman’s money. The witness of Elisha’s integrity became, for Gehazi, not a standard to emulate but an opportunity to exploit — the space Elisha’s refusal created was immediately filled by Gehazi’s greed. Communities where leaders model sacrificial integrity create both an example to follow and a gap that corrupt insiders can exploit. The lesson is not that leaders should compromise their integrity to prevent Gehazi-moves; it is that proximity to prophetic ministry does not automatically produce prophetic character.
20.christology: Gehazi’s greed-driven exploitation of Naaman’s generosity represents the pattern of Simon the Sorcerer in Acts 8:18–24 — the attempt to monetize the Spirit’s power for personal gain, which Peter calls “the poison of bitterness and the bond of iniquity.” Both Gehazi and Simon are insiders who try to convert spiritual access into material advantage; both receive the same withering response (leprosy; “may your silver perish with you”). The contrast with Naaman — the outsider who left healed and confessing — underlines that kingdom membership is defined not by proximity to the man of God but by the humble reception of YHWH’s gift without mercenary calculation. The leprosy that leaves Naaman and clings to Gehazi is the enacted parable of the reversal that Isaiah announced: “the first will be last and the last will be first” (Matt 20:16).
